perturbation.original_text: Officials, employees and agents of Authorized Person having access to any confidential information obtained pursuant to this agreement shall be subjec t to all federal and state laws, and regulations , and will comply with limitations on use, treatment, and safeguarding of data under the following laws, regulations and agreements : K.S.A. 44- 714(e) , and amendments thereto ; the Privacy Act of 1974 (5 U.S.C. 552a), as amended by the Computer Matching and Privacy Protection Act of 1988, related Office of Management and Budget guidelines, the Federal Information Security Management Act of 2002 (44 U.S.C. § 3541, et seq.), and related National Institute of Standards and Technology guidelines ; SSA’s “Electronic Information Exchange Security Requirements and Procedures for State and Local Agencies Exchanging Electronic Information with the Social Security Administration ” (TSSR v7) ; the US Department of Health and Human Services requirements relating to National Directory of New Hires data ; and the Information Exchange Agreement between the Social Security Administration and the State of Kansas Department of Labor .
perturbation.changed_text: Officials, employees and agents of Authorized Person having access to any confidential information obtained pursuant to this agreement shall be subject to all federal and state laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit list of laws and regulations regarding data safeguarding and security, the agreement becomes vague about compliance requirements, weakening the enforceability of data protection measures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Information Security Modernization Act (FISMA) of 2014
perturbation.law_explanation: FISMA requires federal agencies and those connected to them to comply with specific security standards and guidelines, including continuous monitoring and risk assessments. Removing the list of relevant laws makes it difficult to ensure compliance with FISMA requirements.

Federal agency responsibilities (a) In General .-The head of each agency shall- (1) be responsible for- (A) providing information security protections commensurate with the risk and magnitude of the harm resulting from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction of- (i) information collected or maintained by or
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Information Security Modernization Act | CISA Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Free Cyber Services Secure by design Secure Our World Shields Up Report A Cyber Issue Share: Federal Information Security Modernization Act The Federal Information Security Modernization Act of 2014 (FISMA 2014) updates the Federal Government's cybersecurity practices by: Codifying Department of Homeland Security (DHS) authority to administer the implementation of information security policies for non-national security federal Executive Branch systems, including providing technical assistance and deploying technologies to such systems; Amending and clarifying the Office of Management and Budget's (OMB) oversight authority over federal agency information security practices; and by Requiring OMB to amend or revise OMB A-130 to "eliminate inefficient and wasteful reporting." Overview FISMA 2014 codifies the Department of Homeland Security’s role in administering the implementation of information security policies for federal Executive Branch civilian agencies, overseeing agencies’ compliance with those policies, and assisting OMB in developing those policies. The legislation provides the Department authority to develop and oversee the implementation of binding operational directives to other agencies, in coordination and consistent with OMB policies and practices. It also: Authorizes DHS to provide operational and technical assistance to other federal Executive Branch civilian agencies at the agency’s request; Places the federal information security incident center within DHS by law; Authorizes DHS technology deployments to other agencies' networks (upon those agencies' request); Directs OMB to revise policies regarding notification of individuals affected by federal agency data breaches; Requires agencies to report major information security incidents as well as data breaches to Congress as they occur and annually; and Simplifies existing FISMA reporting to eliminate inefficient or wasteful reporting while adding new reporting requirements for major information security incidents. The Federal Information Security Modernization Act of 2014 amends the Federal Information Security Management Act of 2002 (FISMA). perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly lists laws and regulations related to data security, including K.S.A. 44-714(e), the Privacy Act of 1974, FISMA, and others. The changed text removes this list, stating only that officials are subject to federal and state laws and regulations. The Law Explanation states FISMA requires compliance with security standards. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explains FISMA codifies DHS authority to administer information security policies and oversee agency compliance. By removing the specific laws, the changed text weakens the enforceability of data protection measures, making it difficult to ensure compliance with FISMA. The original text provided concrete standards that must be met; removing this list makes it harder to determine if the parties are meeting required standards, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Authorized Person agrees to permit KDOL to audit and inspect all Authorized Person’s compliance with these requirements.
perturbation.changed_text: Authorized Person agrees to permit KDOL to audit and inspect all Authorized Person’s compliance with these requirements. This right to audit may be waived by KDOL at its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Adding that KDOL can waive its right to audit compliance undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement. It creates a loophole that weakens KDOL's ability to ensure data security and compliance with relevant regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: K.A.R. 50-2-2 pertains to the confidentiality of unemployment compensation information. By introducing a waiver for audits, the ability to enforce the confidentiality provisions required by this regulation is weakened, potentially leading to unauthorized disclosures of sensitive information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a waiver for audits, potentially weakening the enforceability of confidentiality provisions as described in the Law Explanation, and thus creates a Structural Flaw Legal Contradiction. The added waiver empowers KDOL to selectively enforce compliance, which undermines the original agreement. The Perturbation Explanation supports the assessment that this weakens KDOL's ability to ensure data security and compliance, aligning with the definition of Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction, where the organization of a contract affects its clarity or enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Once confidential information or data obtained hereunder is no longer useful for the purposes described herein, Authorized Person agrees to immediately destroy all such confidential information or data maintained on any information system platform or any form of storage media subject to any schedule of retention established by the Kansas Records Board , in the following manner: Confidential information obtained pursuant to this agreement, and any paper material generated therefrom, such as copies, photo impressions, computer printouts, notes, and work papers, must be destroyed by burning or shredding.
perturbation.changed_text: Once confidential information or data obtained hereunder is no longer useful for the purposes described herein, Authorized Person agrees to destroy such confidential information or data. The method of destruction will be determined by the Authorized Person.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the mandated destruction methods (burning, shredding, electromagnetic erasure) for confidential information and giving the Authorized Person discretion, the agreement becomes weaker in safeguarding data. This introduces uncertainty and potential non-compliance with data security best practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: IRS Publication 1075 outlines detailed requirements for safeguarding federal tax information (FTI). By allowing the Authorized Person to choose their own method of destruction, the agreement risks violating the stringent media sanitization and destruction standards mandated by the IRS, potentially leading to unauthorized disclosure of FTI.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 - Taxpayer Advocate Service An official website of the U.S. Govt All Categories All Categories NTA Blog Success Stories Tax News Tax Professionals Tax Tips All Year All Year 2010 2012 2013 2014 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 Order By : Author Title Post ID Date Random Order : Asc Desc
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies precise methods for destroying confidential information (burning, shredding). The changed text removes these mandated methods and gives the Authorized Person discretion in choosing the method of destruction. The Law Explanation states that this risks violating stringent media sanitization and destruction standards, potentially leading to unauthorized disclosure of FTI. Scraped Law Snippet 1 alludes to encoded data, which would likely fall under mandated protection by IRS Publication 1075. Therefore, by removing the mandated destruction methods, the changed text introduces a contradiction and weakens data safeguarding, increasing the risk of non-compliance and unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: Information provided hereunder, or identifiable information derived therefrom , shall not be copied, downloaded or otherwise placed in any format, including working papers, which may become a public record pursuant to the Kansas Open Records Act (KORA).
perturbation.changed_text: Information provided hereunder, or identifiable information derived therefrom , shall not be copied, downloaded or otherwise placed in any format. However, this restriction does not apply to working papers.
perturbation.explanation: Exempting working papers from the restriction on copying and downloading confidential information directly conflicts with the intent of KORA to prevent such information from becoming a public record, and increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: KORA mandates that government records are open to the public unless specifically exempted. By allowing working papers containing confidential information to be copied or downloaded, the agreement creates a loophole that could result in the unauthorized release of sensitive data that should be protected under KORA's exceptions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: GriefShare Group begins Jan. 24 at Oak Ridge church FAITH GriefShare Group begins Jan. 24 at Oak Ridge church The Oak Ridger Central Baptist Church in Oak Ridge will again offer an in-person GriefShare support group beginning Wednesday, Jan. 24. The meetings will be held each Wednesday from 6 to 7:30 p.m. for for 13 weeks at Central Baptist Church, 130 Providence Road, Oak Ridge. GriefShare is a network of 15,000 churches world-wide equipped to offer grief support groups. It is non-denominational and features biblical concepts for healing from the loss of your loved one. According to a news release, there are three key components to the program: A video seminar featuring leading grief recovery experts Your support group, where there is discussion about the weekly video content and you can share your story if so desired A workbook that reinforces the weekly session topics. The group facilitators will be Jack Bailey, a member of Central Baptist Church, who has experienced the loss of his wife and younger sister in the past five years, as well as Marie Westfall, who lost her adult son. Because past groups have tended to be mostly women, a separate men’s group will also be offered if there is sufficient interest. Register at GriefShare.org or the church webpage, www.orcbc.org . Feel free to call the church office at 865-483-7495 with any questions. Need a break? Play the USA TODAY Daily Crossword Puzzle.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that exempting working papers from restrictions conflicts with KORA's intent to prevent confidential information from becoming a public record. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that allowing working papers to be copied creates a loophole that could result in unauthorized data release that should be protected under KORA's exceptions. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, the explanation provided shows a clear contradiction, as the modified text weakens KORA's intent. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is irrelevant.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement does not grant or authorize the Authorized Person to have unescorted direct access to KDOL systems . Additionally, execution of this agreement alone does not fulfill all requirements to permit Authorized Person access to confidential informa tion.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement grants the Authorized Person access to KDOL systems. Additional requirements must be satisfied.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that this agreement grants access to KDOL systems, while also stating 'additional requirements must be satisfied', the agreement becomes contradictory and ambiguous. It is uncertain under which conditions the agreement grants access and under which conditions is additional requirements needed.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Privacy Act mandates that agencies establish safeguards to protect individuals' information. Granting system access based on a vague and contradictory clause undermines these safeguards, making it easier for unauthorized access to occur, which is in violation of the principle of minimizing access to PII.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 5 / PART I / CHAPTER 5 / SUBCHAPTER II / § 552a Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 5 USC 552a : Records maintained on individuals Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 5-GOVERNMENT ORGANIZATION AND EMPLOYEES PART I-THE AGENCIES GENERALLY CHAPTER 5-ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE SUBCHAPTER II-ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Codification Amendments Change of Name Effective Date Short Title Termination Date Regulations Executive Documents §552a. Records maintained on individuals (a) Definitions .-For purposes of this section- (1) the term "agency" means agency as defined in section 552(e) 1 of this title; (2) the term "individual" means a citizen of the United States or an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence; (3) the term "maintain" includes maintain, collect, use, or disseminate; (4) the term "record" means any item, collection, or grouping of information about an individual that is maintained by an agency, including, but not limited to, his education, financial transactions, medical history, and criminal or employment history and that contains his name, or the identifying number, symbol, or other identifying particular assigned to the individual, such as a finger or voice print or a photograph; (5) the term "system of records" means a group of any records under the control of any agency from which information is retrieved by the name of the individual or by some identifying number, symbol, or other identifying particular assigned to the individual; (6) the term "statistical record" means a record in a system of records maintained for statistical research or reporting purposes only and not used in whole or in part in making any determination about an identifiable individual, except as provided by section 8 of title 13 ; (7) the term "routine use" means, with respect to the disclosure of a record, the use of such record for a purpose which is compatible with the purpose for which it was collected; (8) the term "matching program"- (A) means any computerized comparison of- (i) two or more automated systems of records or a system of records with non-Federal records for the purpose of- (I) establishing or verifying the eligibility of, or continuing compliance with statutory and regulatory requirements by, applicants for, recipients or beneficiaries of, participants in, or providers of services with respect to, cash or in-kind assistance or payments under Federal benefit programs, or (II) recouping payments or delinquent debts under such Federal benefit programs, or (ii) two or more automated Federal personnel or payroll systems of records
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Office of Privacy and Civil Liberties | Privacy Act of 1974 Skip to main content Privacy Act of 1974 Share Facebook X LinkedIn Email The Privacy Act of 1974, as amended, 5 U.S.C. § 552a , establishes a code of fair information practices that governs the collection, maintenance, use, and dissemination of information about individuals that is maintained in systems of records by federal agencies. A system of records is a group of records under the control of an agency from which information is retrieved by the name of the individual or by some identifier assigned to the individual. The Privacy Act requires that agencies give the public notice of their systems of records by publication in the Federal Register. Click here to see a list of DOJ systems of records and their Federal Register citations . The Privacy Act prohibits the disclosure of a record about an individual from a system of records absent the written consent of the individual, unless the disclosure is pursuant to one of twelve statutory exceptions. The Act also provides individuals with a means by which to seek access to and amendment of their records, and sets forth various agency record-keeping requirements. Overview of the Privacy Act The " Overview of the Privacy Act of 1974, 2020 Edition " is a comprehensive treatise of existing Privacy Act case law. Any questions regarding the Overview may be directed to the Office of Privacy and Civil Liberties staff. Updated October 4, 2022
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly denies immediate access to KDOL systems, stating that 'this agreement does not grant or authorize' such access, while requiring further steps for confidential information. The changed text directly contradicts this by stating 'This agreement grants the Authorized Person access to KDOL systems.' The Law Explanation suggests this undermines safeguards for individual information, potentially violating the Privacy Act's principle of minimizing access to PII. Snippet 2 says the Privacy Act of 1974 'establishes a code of fair information practices that governs the collection, maintenance, use, and dissemination of information about individuals that is maintained in systems of records by federal agencies.' By granting access outright, the changed text weakens the original security measures, potentially leading to unauthorized access.
